在过去的几个月里，Twitter一直在努力将其俱乐部的音频聊天室功能，空格带到iOS上的用户，截至上个月，Android。逻辑下一场空间用于出现的空间将在网络上，而且不出所料，Twitter正在研究浏览器的功能，该公司确认濒临。
 我们决定在从App研究员Jane Manchun Wong看到此推文后向Twitter询问桌面上的空间，显示在网上查看时的空间预览卡看起来像什么样的空间。
  而且Twitter本身也没有完全秘密开发该功能。 Twitter Spaces开发人员发布了一些设计空间的介绍屏幕上周五在网上看起来的设计。
  鉴于现在只在移动设备上可用，将其带到网络上将是一个重要的扩展。更广泛的范围可以帮助推特与俱乐部竞争竞争，它仍然只在iOS上（虽然Android版本正在路上）。它将带来Twitter，更接近奇怪的奇偶频，即昨天推出其社会音频室的特色，舞台渠道，舞台渠道在不和谐的所有平台上。
 LinkedIn，Mark Cuban，Slack和Spotify也在俱乐部的现场音频功能上工作，而Facebook据报道，有一个开发。
note_en:
Over the past several months, Twitter has been working hard to bring its Clubhouse-like audio chat rooms feature, Spaces, to users on iOS and,  as of last month, Android. A logical next place for Spaces to show up would be on the web, and unsurprisingly, Twitter is working on the feature for browsers, the company confirmed to  The Verge.
 We decided to ask Twitter about Spaces on desktop after seeing  this tweet from app researcher Jane Manchun Wong, which shows what Spaces preview cards could look like when viewed on the web.
  And Twitter  itself hasn’t been entirely secret about developing the feature, either. A Twitter Spaces developer posted  some  designs of how a Space’s intro screen might look on the web last Friday.
  Given that Spaces is only available on mobile right now, bringing it to the web would be a significant expansion. And that broader reach could help Twitter in its race to compete with Clubhouse, which is still only on iOS (though an Android version  is on the way). It would also bring Twitter in closer parity to Discord, which just yesterday launched its social audio rooms feature,  Stage Channels, on all platforms where Discord is available.
 LinkedIn,  Mark Cuban,  Slack, and  Spotify are also working on Clubhouse-like live audio features, while  Facebook reportedly has one in development.
title: Twitter确认空格即将到来桌面Web浏览器
title_en: Twitter confirms Spaces are coming to desktop web browsers

女孩们唱“我们想要保罗！” - 但它不是McCartney，他们正在昏迷。这是1961年的夏天，披头士乐队仍然超过一年，距离录音爱情我做了。相反，心脏悸动杜穴是来自加拿大的19岁的孩子，名叫保罗·安卡。在大西洋城市木板路上，女士们排队才能获得签名;其中一些也给予或接受一个吻。相机跟随年轻的明星后台，进入梳妆室。音乐会即将开始，所以安卡连衣裙匆匆忙忙。我们在内衣中看到他。后来，他坦率地说出学校“一个沉重的孩子”，并决心成为艺人的决心。他失去了35磅。
 “你必须有吸引力，”他说，几乎直接看进相机。 “你必须看起来像是在展示业务 - 如果你不这样做，你就不会成功。”
 这种亲密的纪录片，以anka最大的命中命名，被称为孤独的男孩，并由加拿大电影制片人制作和共同指导，该电影制剂应该更好地知道：罗马克罗特。
 在2012年去世的克罗特是在电影制作中的一系列创新中所知。他正处于CinémaVérité的最前沿，通过像孤独男孩一样的电影以及传奇钢琴球员Gleng Gould的一对短纪录片，另一个在伊戈尔斯特拉伐克斯基的生活中带来了另一个。后来，克罗特开创性的多屏电影制作和共同创立的IMAX，该公司将为世界各地的观众提供巨大的电影体验。沿途，他制作了一部电影，因为他正在制作2001年的斯坦利库巴里克：一个太空奥德赛加克罗迪纳刚刚碰巧给乔治卢卡斯为“力量”的想法。
 但最重要的是，罗马克罗特是一个直观地了解视觉讲故事的元素的风险接地者，召回电影制片人斯蒂芬低，他们经常与克罗特（他的父亲，Colin Low一样）合作。虽然克罗特制造了戏剧以及纪录片，但有一些关于后一种格式的东西，让他着迷。低于它，他“喜欢讲述真正的人的真正故事”。
 虽然这部电影制片人的故事已经褪色了一点时间，但克罗特对数十家电影制作人以及工艺本身产生了非常真实的影响。今天，他可能是电影历史最忽视的又有影响力的人物之一。
     克罗特于1926年出生于1926年，克罗特去了温尼伯上学，后来赢得了曼尼托大学的哲学硕士学位。当时加拿大没有电影学校，所以他在1949年开始在全国电影委员会的暑期实习时学习。他在1952年指导了他的第一部电影，救援派对。然后，在20世纪50年代后期和早期＆＃39; 60s，在电影世界中发生了激进的东西。突然，纪录片变得更加亲密，更真实。该运动经常被其法国名称呼叫，CinémaVérité。和克罗特，以及在NFB工作的少数同事都处于这一新电影制作的最前沿。 
许多创新的电影制片人已经通过这一点，但是蒙哥罗·弗格森（Lunro Ferguson）全部作为一个年轻人，非常意识到他们的工作是如何革命的。弗格森曾担任数十部电影的动画总监，他的父亲Graeme Ferguson，与克罗特共同创立的IMAX。 （Ferguson也是克罗特的侄子;格雷梅的妹妹，珍妮特，是克罗特的寡妇。）这一新的，更亲密的讲故事形式是可能的三个新的技术，所有这些都在现场同时出现在现场，弗格森告诉Ars。第一个开发是16毫米电影膜，允许容易地手持足够小的相机。第二种是质量好的便携式音录设备，如Nagra Reel-to-reel卷录录。第三个是变焦镜头，允许电影制作人从宽视图切换到特写而不改变镜头。
 “你从一部七八个人的电影船员到了两个人 - 一个相机运营商和一个声音男人，”弗格森召回。 “所以你可以在你的电影制作中更自发，并且真的试图捕捉现实。”
 截至目前，大多数纪录片都被脚本，解释了Albert Ohayon，NFB的策展人。场景被排练;一切都提前计划。随着CinémaVérité的曙光，“我们突然我们拥有这个便携式拍摄设备，我们能够继续找到位置，而且只是电影的东西，就像他们发生一样，”他说。 “我认为时代的电影制作人，包括罗马克罗特，他曾在这个非常扼杀的时代开始，其中一切都必须提前准备，突然感受到这种解放。”
    Kroitor的早期短纪录片之一，Paul Tomkowicz：Street-Railway Switchman（1953），与听起来一样亲密。相机遵循一个64岁的波兰语移民，因为他在温尼伯的一个幸福冬天的夜晚维护了路上的轨道，扫过了雪并腌制了轨道。 Tomkowicz从未看过镜头，但有时它一定是不超过一个手臂的长度。他讲述了他的故事。 “我知道像我自己的花园这样的轨道，”他说。在最后的场景中，日光返回。他转过来，他居过一家咖啡和早餐，感谢剃刀的图像，我们可以看到五个硬煮的鸡蛋，三个香肠和六片面包。
 在孤独的男孩和Tomkowicz电影中看到的亲密关系再次在一部关于古尔德的电影克罗特转起了古尔德，称为Glenn Gould：关闭记录和Glenn Gould：在记录中（从1959年起）。它也在Stravinsky电影中，简单地标题为Stravinsky（1965）。在Gleng Gould薄膜的第一个古尔格电影中，我们认为在多伦多湖的海岸的湖泊里，我们看到了他家的大钢琴专注于大钢琴 - 但我们也看到他沿着一个乡村车道走了他的牧羊犬班克。在一个点，古尔德坐在他的花园里，坐在他的花园里，回答克罗特提出的问题 - 但我们也看到他在与同事和广播生产者Franz Kraemer的谈话中。他们的戏剧是完全没有计划的，在脚本纪录片时代，肯定感到革命。 （会发生什么，古怪的缪斯，如果孩子被养育没有玛丽有一点羊肉，但与Schoenberg一起？这个孩子会对十二个色调造成亲和力吗？）
 “感觉自发 - 你不知道接下来会发生什么，”弗格森说。 “这一事实中有一种兴奋，你知道它被枪杀了。”这不是计划的;什么事情都可能发生。”
 在第二部电影中，我们在曼哈顿的一室公寓中看到了Gould唱片;相机有时在古尔德，有时在相邻的控制室的工程师的Bevy上。 “我喜欢古尔通正在玩的顺序，他们录音，”Ohayon说：“但相机不是古尔德;相机是在工程师身上，谁在彼此之间，谈论他们的周末 - 直到他们意识到相机在他们身上，他们开始谈论录音会话。“ 
Stravinsky电影同样充满了揭示事件，就像作曲家在德国汉堡的酒店房间安置在他的酒店房间，小说家弗拉基米尔·纳比科夫队进入了访问。 （一个感官克罗特和他的共同董事Wolf Koenig知道Nabokov将通过 - 否则他们非常幸运能够在那一刻在酒店房间里设立的相机。）有趣的是，CinémaVérité没有要求电影制作人消失;相反，他们经常出现在故事​​的周边 - 有时在框架内。在一个点，斯特拉斯基建议克罗特和科诺伊太某东西 - 他抓住了一个俄语 - 英语词典，他总是带着太“勤奋”，他说。他邀请了两款电影制作人放松并加入他们喝一杯。
   作为这些电影的创新，克罗特的1960年纪录片宇宙，与Colin Low联合，更加开创性。这部电影是标题的表明，在宇宙之旅中取代观众 - 或者当时所知的宇宙。它经常与Carl Sagan的Cosmos TV系列进行比较，尽管宇宙在二十年内宇宙之日期宇宙之日期。宇宙在多伦多北部大卫邓莲天文台观察的一夜观察的天文学家唐纳德·麦克风。它还具有广泛的行星和卫星，星星和星系的剧烈动画。
 在宇宙的敬畏之中是晚斯坦利库布里克。 Kubrick在2001年正准备工作时筛选了这部电影，“他绝对喜欢它，”Ohayon说。 “他联系了NFB，几位在包括罗马克罗特在内的电影中工作的几个人，并要求他们在2001年与他一起工作。” Kroitor拒绝了，虽然Kubrick聘请Wally Gentleman，他们在宇宙的光学效果上工作，以帮助2001年。他还聘请了叙述宇宙的道格拉斯雨，成为HAL 9000电脑的声音。
  克罗特与乔治卢卡斯的连接是更多的环形交叉路口，但就像令人着迷。卢卡斯南加州大学的一名学生筛选了许多NFB电影，包括另一张叫做21-87（1964年）的实验工作，另一个加拿大电影制片人Arthur Lipsett。这部电影实际上是图像和声音的蒙太奇，它包括来自其他薄膜的未使用的夹子 - 包括从克罗卡所代为人机的薄膜的出口。在这片赛段中，克罗特听到与神经科学家和人工智能先驱沃伦麦卡洛克交谈。显然对麦克洛尔的断言，人类仅仅是复杂的机器，克罗特说：“很多人觉得在沉思的自然中，以及与其他生物的沟通，他们意识到某种力量，或者在这方面意识到这一点我们在我们面前看到的面具，他们称之为上帝。“
    卢卡斯被迷住了。和十三年后，达斯·瓦德将用力扼杀他的敌人，而卢克将用它来消除死亡之星。 （哦，“2187”在原始星球大战片中展示了莱娅公主的手机号码，并且再次作为Finn的Stormtrooper指定-FN-2187-FN-2187-in Force Avakens。）
  在迷宫中，在蒙特利尔博览会67的多屏幕上，专门为一个多屏剧院创造了哪种克罗纳与霍尔·奥尔诺进行了专门的。这部电影，地球上的人类的一种快照，被射杀了世界：我们看到美国的大平原和非洲雨林;我们被带到希腊和印度和柬埔寨;我们看到一座骆驼的大篷车在沙漠中走过沙漠。我们在伦敦举行的温斯顿丘吉尔的葬礼，在莫斯科举行的芭蕾舞课上的年轻女孩，以及苏联宇航员培训的空间发射 - 全部全部颜色。五个屏幕中的每个屏幕上的运动图像有时齐声流动;有时它们会提供独立的鸽子。 （虽然在NFB的网站上观看，但在电脑或电视上看到它肯定不会捕获访问者将享受的超大经验。）
 “迷宫是一个真正的杰作，”斯蒂芬低。 “电影委员会值得他赞同这部电影关于人类;关于生活，关于生命的阶段。这是一个非常艺术上的风险，复杂，挑战性 - 但他把它拉出来了。“ 
让迷宫似乎给了克罗特的一种味道，以获得更大的视觉演示。 “像世博会67这样的多屏幕的问题是你必须拥有不同的投影仪，必须完全同步，”弗格森说。 “那种方式真的很复杂。所以他们想，'让我们只是建造一个大型投影机。'这就是IMAX的出现。“
 IMAX系统采用特殊的摄像机和专用投影仪。在这两种机器中，薄膜通过侧向送入，具有三个标准的65mm帧，构成单个IMAX框架。 （宽高比为4：3，与那个时代的电视相同 - 但是通过更多的分辨率。）克罗特和格雷梅弗格森公司创立了多屏公司，该公司将被称为IMAX的公司与拥有当地印刷公司的Robert Kerr，后来带来了工程师比尔肖。克罗特制作了第一个IMAX薄膜，虎子 - 一种迷宫续集 - 在日本大阪的Expo 70首映。接下来的其他超大电影，包括海尔哥伦比亚（1982年），由弗格森与克罗特作为作家和合作制作人，其中记录了第一个航天飞机特派团;和过渡（1986），第一个3D IMAX膜。
 如果他曾经如此倾向，凯托特就可以在好莱坞定居，成为主流电影业务的一部分 - 但这种举动不呼吁。这不仅仅是因为他喜欢在蒙特利尔和周围地区的亲密的朋友和同事附近，他花了大部分成年生活，尽管这肯定是其中的一部分。他只是找到了好莱坞，以及围绕它的名人文化，成为“令人厌恶”，因为弗格森把它放了。另外，“他想在加拿大制作电影。”
 当时的加拿大电影场景可能是一个小池塘，但最终让克罗特成为一条非常大的鱼。 Kroitor与NFB持续数十年的协会;在20世纪60年代和＆＃39; 70年代，他在那里监督了戏剧性的生产，后来他作为一名执行制造商致力于，看到最值得的项目得到了绿灯。电影板，其部分，欣赏克罗特的视野和能源。
 “NFB在世界上是独一无二的，”低。 “它给了这些年轻人有机会制作这些魔法电影。”
   Kroitor的最终指导项目是1991年滚石音乐会影片，在Max-A项目被分歧和内脏困扰着。低调说乐队成员不能同意董事，并且工作最终违背克罗特的腿部落入克罗特的腿 - 尽管他不是石头粉丝。 “我不认为他对滚石留下了非常深刻的印象，”低召回。克罗特希望这部电影会讲述某种故事;乐队想要一个直接的音乐会电影。虽然克罗特可能是无动态的，但像罗杰·埃伯特这样的批评者被吹走了。 “我的经验中没有其他音乐电影已经让眼睛和耳朵变得越来越多，吸引着我们进入了一个摇滚乐音乐会的感觉和质地，”着名的评论家在他的评论中写道。
 低记得克罗特不仅仅是一位辉煌的电影制片人和讲故事者，而且作为一名不断寻求新的人的讲述者。 “他对过去发生的事情并不感兴趣，”低点说。 “他想尝试。”他可能不是最容易与之相处的人 - 他有时可能会多刺，他肯定很苛刻。 “但每个人都受益于罗马的勇气和他的聪明才智。他创造性地，在技术上造成了疯狂的风险 - 每个人都受益于此。“ 
对于那些好奇的，今天只有两个选择才能追赶克罗特的工作 -  YouTube和NFB的Web档案。 但是，如果你现在花时间观看像宇宙这样的东西，近60年后，它仍然很容易看出为什么这个较鲜为人知的加拿大电影制片人迷住了一些最大，最受尊敬的电影名字。  Dan Falk（@danfalk）是一名位于多伦多的科学记者。 他的书包括莎士比亚的科学，寻找时间。
note_en:
Girls chant “We want Paul!”—but it’s not McCartney they’re swooning over. This is the summer of 1961, and the Beatles are still more than a year away from recording  Love Me Do. Instead, the heart throb du jour is a 19-year-old kid from Canada named Paul Anka. At the Atlantic City boardwalk, the ladies line up to get autographs; some of them also give, or receive, a kiss. The camera follows the young star backstage and into the dressing room. The concert is about to start, so Anka dresses hurriedly. We see him in his underwear. Later, he speaks candidly of being “a heavy kid” in school and of his determination to become what entertainers were expected to be. He lost 35 pounds.
 “You’ve got to have appeal,” he says, looking almost directly into the camera. “You’ve got to look like you’re in show business—if you don’t, you’re not going to make it.”
 This intimate documentary, named for one of Anka’s biggest hits, is called   Lonely Boy, and it was produced and co-directed by a Canadian filmmaker who ought to be much better known:  Roman Kroitor.
 Kroitor, who died in 2012, was known for a string of innovations in movie-making. He was at the forefront of the  cinéma vérité movement, typified by films like  Lonely Boy as well as a pair of short documentaries on the legendary piano player Glenn Gould, and another that brings viewers into the life of Igor Stravinsky. Later, Kroitor pioneered multi-screen filmmaking and co-founded  IMAX, the company that would deliver a giant-sized cinematic experience to viewers around the world. And along the way, he made a film that inspired Stanley Kubrick as he was making   2001: A Space Odyssey—plus Kroitor just happened to give George Lucas the idea for “the Force.”
 But above all else, Roman Kroitor was a risk-taker who intuitively understood the elements of visual storytelling, recalls filmmaker  Stephen Low, who often collaborated with Kroitor (as did his father,  Colin Low). While Kroitor made dramas as well as documentaries, there was something about the latter format that fascinated him. As Low puts it, he “loved telling real stories that celebrated real people.”
 And though the story of this filmmaker has faded a bit over time, Kroitor made a very real impact on dozens of filmmakers, and on the craft itself. Today, he may be one of film history’s most overlooked yet influential figures.
     Born in 1926 in Saskatchewan, Kroitor went to school in Winnipeg and later earned a master’s degree in philosophy and psychology from the University of Manitoba. There were no film schools in Canada at the time, so he learned on the job, beginning with a summer internship at the  National Film Board in 1949. He directed his first film,  Rescue Party, in 1952. Then, in the late 1950s and early '60s, something radical happened in the world of film. Suddenly, documentaries became more intimate, more real. The movement is often called by its French name,  cinéma vérité. And Kroitor, along with a handful of colleagues working at the NFB, were at the forefront of this new wave of movie-making.
 Many of those innovative filmmakers have passed on by this point, but  Munro Ferguson, who witnessed it all as a youngster, is very conscious of just how revolutionary their work was. Ferguson has served as animation director for dozens of films, and his father, Graeme Ferguson, co-founded IMAX with Kroitor. (Ferguson is also Kroitor’s nephew; Graeme’s sister, Janet, is Kroitor’s widow.) This new, more intimate form of storytelling was made possible by three new pieces of technology, all of which appeared on the scene just about simultaneously, Ferguson tells Ars. The first development was 16mm motion picture film, which allowed for cameras that were small enough to be easily hand-held. The second was good-quality, portable sound-recording equipment, like the  Nagra reel-to-reel tape recorder. The third was the zoom lens, allowing the filmmaker to switch from wide views to close-ups without changing lenses.
 “You went from a film crew of seven or eight people down to two people—a camera operator and a sound man,” recalls Ferguson. “So you could be much more spontaneous in your filmmaking, and really try to capture reality as it is.”
 Up to this point, most documentaries were scripted, explains Albert Ohayon, a curator at the NFB. Scenes were rehearsed; everything was planned in advance. With the dawn of  cinéma vérité, “all of a sudden we have this portable filming equipment, and we’re able to go on location, and just film things as they happen,” he says. “I think the filmmakers of the era, including Roman Kroitor, who had started in this very stifling era in which everything had to be prepared in advance, all of a sudden felt this liberation.”
    One of Kroitor’s early short documentaries,   Paul Tomkowicz: Street-railway Switchman (1953), is as intimate as it sounds. The camera follows a 64-year-old Polish immigrant as he maintains the streetcar tracks on a blustery winter night in Winnipeg, sweeping away the snow and salting the tracks. Tomkowicz never looks at the camera, though at times it must have been no more than an arm’s length away. He tells his story in voiceover. “I know the tracks like my own garden,” he says. In the final scene, the daylight has returned. His shift over, he settles into a diner for coffee and a breakfast, which, thanks to the razor-sharp images, we can see consists of five hard-boiled eggs, three sausages, and six slices of bread.
 The intimacy seen in  Lonely Boy and in the Tomkowicz film turns up again in the pair of films Kroitor made about Gould, called   Glenn Gould: Off the Record and   Glenn Gould: On the Record (both from 1959). It’s also there in the Stravinsky film, simply titled   Stravinsky (1965). In the first of the Glenn Gould films, we see Gould hammering intently on the grand piano in his home, by the shores of Lake Simcoe, north of Toronto—but we also see him walking his collie, Banquo, along a country lane. At one point, Gould, seated in his garden by the edge of the lake, answers questions posed by Kroitor—but we also see him in conversation with fellow musician and radio producer Franz Kraemer. Their banter is entirely unplanned, which, after an era of scripted documentaries, surely felt revolutionary. (What would happen, Gould muses, if a child were raised not with  Mary Had a Little Lamb but with Schoenberg? Would the kid develop an affinity for the twelve-tone scale?)
 “It feels spontaneous—you don’t know what’s going to happen next,” says Ferguson. “There’s a kind of excitement from the fact that you know it was shot ‘live.’ This isn’t planned at all; anything could happen.”
 In the second film, we see Gould recording in a studio in Manhattan; the camera is sometimes on Gould and sometimes on the bevy of engineers in the adjacent control room. “I love the sequence where Gould is playing, and they’re recording,” says Ohayon, “but the camera isn’t on Gould; the camera is on the engineers, who are yakking amongst each other, talking about their weekend—until they realize the camera is on them, and they start talking about the recording session again.”
 The Stravinsky film is similarly full of revealing happenings—like the scene where the composer is settling into his hotel room in Hamburg, Germany, and the novelist Vladimir Nabokov drops in for a visit. (One senses that Kroitor and his co-director  Wolf Koenig knew that Nabokov was going to come by—or else they were very, very lucky to have the camera set up in the hotel room at that moment.) Interestingly,  cinéma vérité does not require the filmmaker to disappear; rather, they often appear at the periphery of the story—and sometimes within the frame. At one point, Stravinsky suggests Kroitor and Koenig are being too  something—he grabs a Russian-English dictionary that he always travels with—too “diligent,” he says. He invites the two filmmakers to relax and join them for a drink.
   As innovative as those films were, Kroitor’s 1960 documentary   Universe, co-directed with Colin Low, was even more groundbreaking. The film, as the title suggests, takes viewers on a tour of the universe—or what was known of the universe at that time. It has often been compared to Carl Sagan’s  Cosmos TV series, though  Universe pre-dates  Cosmos by two decades.  Universe follows astronomer Donald MacRae over the course of a night observing at the  David Dunlap Observatory, north of Toronto. It also features remarkably sophisticated animation of planets and moons, stars and galaxies.
 Among those in awe of  Universe was the late Stanley Kubrick. Kubrick screened the film while he was preparing to work on  2001, “and he absolutely loved it,” says Ohayon. “He contacted the NFB, and several of the people who had worked on the film, including Roman Kroitor, and asked them to come down and work with him on  2001.” Kroitor declined, though Kubrick hired Wally Gentleman, who worked on  Universe’s optical effects, to help with  2001. He also hired Douglas Rain, who narrates  Universe, to be the voice of the HAL 9000 computer.
  Kroitor’s connection to George Lucas is more roundabout, but just as fascinating. While a student at the University of Southern California, Lucas screened a number of NFB films, including an experimental work called   21-87 (1964), by another Canadian filmmaker,  Arthur Lipsett. The film is actually a montage of images and sounds, and it includes unused clips from other films—including an outtake from a film that Kroitor had made called  The Human Machine. In this snippet, Kroitor is heard talking with neuroscientist and artificial intelligence pioneer Warren McCulloch. Apparently responding to McCullough’s assertion that human beings are merely complex machines, Kroitor says: “Many people feel that in the contemplation of nature, and in communication with other living things, they become aware of some kind of force, or something, behind this apparent mask which we see in front of us, and they call it God.”
    Lucas was mesmerized. And thirteen years later, Darth Vader would use the Force to strangle his enemies while Luke would use it to obliterate the Death Star. (Oh, and “2187”  shows up as Princess Leia’s cell number in the original  Star Wars film, and again as Finn’s stormtrooper designation—FN-2187—in  The Force Awakens.)
  In the  Labyrinth, which Kroitor co-directed with Colin Low and Hugh O’Connor, was created specifically for a multi-screen theater at Montreal’s Expo 67. The film, a kind of snapshot of humanity on planet Earth, was shot all over the world: we see America’s Great Plains and African rainforests; we’re taken to Greece and India and Cambodia; we see a caravan of camels making their way across a desert. We catch glimpses of Winston Churchill’s funeral in London, young girls at a ballet lesson in Moscow, and Soviet cosmonauts training for a space launch—all in full color. The moving images on each of the five screens sometimes flow in unison; sometimes they offer independent vignettes. (Though viewable on the NFB’s website, seeing it on a computer or a TV surely doesn’t quite capture the oversized experience that visitors to Expo would have enjoyed.)
 “ Labyrinth was a real masterpiece,” says Stephen Low. “The film board trusted him to make this film about humanity; about life, about the stages of life. It’s a very artistically risky, complex, challenging thing—but he pulled it off.”
 Making  Labyrinth seems to have given Kroitor a taste for ever-larger visual presentation. “The problem with multi-screen like they were doing at Expo 67 is that you had to have different projectors that had to be perfectly synchronized,” says Ferguson. “It was really complicated to do things that way. So they thought, ‘Let’s just build one big projector.’ And that’s how IMAX emerged.”
 The IMAX system employed special cameras and dedicated projectors. In both machines, the film is fed through sideways, with three standard 65mm frames making up a single IMAX frame. (The aspect ratio works out to 4:3, the same as television of that era—but with a heck of a lot more resolution.) Kroitor and Graeme Ferguson founded Multi-Screen Corporation, the company that would become known as  IMAX, along with Robert Kerr, who owned a local printing company, and later brought on engineer Bill Shaw. Kroitor produced the first IMAX film,   Tiger Child—a kind of sequel to  Labyrinth—which premiered at Expo 70 in Osaka, Japan. Further oversized films followed, including   Hail Columbia (1982), directed by Ferguson with Kroitor as writer and co-producer, which documented the first space shuttle mission; and   Transitions (1986), the first 3D IMAX film.
 Had he been so inclined, Kroitor could have settled in Hollywood and become part of the mainstream movie business—but such a move held no appeal. That wasn’t just because he liked being near his close-knit group of friends and colleagues in and around Montreal, where he spent most of his adult life, although that was surely a part of it. He simply found Hollywood, and the celebrity culture that swirled around it, to be “distasteful,” as Ferguson puts it. Plus, “He wanted to make films in Canada.”
 The Canadian film scene at that time may have been a small pond, but it ultimately allowed Kroitor to be a very large fish. Kroitor’s association with the NFB lasted decades; in the 1960s and '70s, he supervised dramatic production there, and he later worked as an executive producer, seeing that the most deserving projects got the green light. The film board, for its part, appreciated Kroitor’s vision and energy.
 “The NFB was unique in the world,” says Low. “It gave these young guys a chance to make these magical films.”
   Kroitor’s final directing project was the 1991 Rolling Stones concert film,   At the Max—a project plagued by disagreements and infighting. Low says the members of the band couldn’t agree on a director, and the job eventually fell into Kroitor’s lap more or less by accident—even though he was not a Stones fan. “I don’t think he was very impressed by the Rolling Stones,” Low recalls. Kroitor was hoping the film would tell some sort of story; the band wanted a straight-up concert film. While Kroitor may have been unmoved, critics like Roger Ebert were blown away. “No other musical film in my experience has so overwhelmed the eyes and ears, drawing us into the feeling and texture of a rock concert,” the famous critic wrote in his review.
 Low remembers Kroitor not just as a brilliant filmmaker and storyteller, but as someone who was constantly seeking the new, the untested. “He wasn’t interested in what had happened in the past,” says Low. “He wanted to experiment.” He might not have been the easiest person to get along with—he could sometimes be prickly, and he was certainly demanding. “But everyone benefited from Roman’s courage and his ingenuity. He took crazy risks, creatively and technically—and everyone benefited from that.”
 For those curious, today there are really only two choices to catch up on Kroitor’s work—YouTube and the  NFB’s web archive. But if you take the time to watch something like  Universe now, nearly 60 years after its release, it remains easy to see why this lesser-known Canadian filmmaker captivated some of the biggest and most revered names in film.
 Dan Falk (@danfalk)  is a science journalist based in Toronto. His books include The Science of Shakespeare  and In Search of Time .
title: 被遗忘的董事给了我们力量，启发了2001年，改变了电影
title_en: The forgotten director who gave us The Force, inspired 2001, and changed film

纽约市大都市运输当局上秋季发布了一个数字版本的地铁地图，旨在让骑手看到网络如何一目了然：火车在实时到他们的车站接近哪个轨道，可以关闭哪些轨道，以及是否任何计划的工作可能会扰乱即将到来的旅行。
  MTA表示，提高数字导航体验的清晰度和易于易于驾驶更多人骑地地铁，包括骑手在令人沮丧地困扰着令人沮丧的轨道关闭和延误时。 “这是一种方式，希望将这些人带回系统，”代理商的首席客户官员Sarah Meyer说。
  MTA是在过去一年中引入或改造其乘客导航工具的几个过境机构之一。
  伦敦的运输或TFL，去年8月推出了一个名为TFL Go的新导航和直播应用程序。洛杉矶县都市交通局，俗称L.A. Metro，上次可能会宣布与运输平台合作，将原子能机构作为该市的官方旅行计划应用程序。
    新平台旨在更加注重用户体验，而不是一些前辈，这是加州大学交通研究所的交通研究所副主任的Juan Mattute表示，这是一些前辈们最初出现在2010年的应用程序商店，并且经常被过境机构忽视。 ， 洛杉矶。 “这些应用程序刚刚偏离偏袒，并最终被从市场上移除，”他说。
    John Gordon（La Metro）营销战略，数字和广告高级总监John Gordon表示，他们的Slick Digital App等旅行替代品如同乘车分享服务等乘坐服务，推动了各种机构，以了解自己平台的相对笨拙。 。
  “过去十年面对很多过境机构的冷水是实现我们需要注意我们的骑手的需求，我们需要建立一个具有竞争力的经验，”他说。 
新平台还必须与流行的应用程序竞争，例如Google地图和Apple地图，多年来已将各机构提供的直播数据组合在最先进的用户体验设计中。但过境机构无法控制这些应用程序的工作方式，或者他们的代表所说的哪种运输方式。
    Transit Bosses表示，开发的专有映射平台意味着代理商可以在他们自己的时间表上提高导航体验，为平台添加唯一的新功能，并获得对用户数据的直接访问，转换博登斯表示。例如，MTA在3月份添加了Covid-19疫苗接种站点定位器，以其交互式图，它在网页而不是应用程序中运行，因此没有智能设备的人可以访问它。 TFL后面的团队说，我们正在考虑引入新功能，帮助乘客更好地导航管道经常迷住地站。
  另一个好处：代理商可以设计自己的应用程序，以推动用户更可持续的运输方式，并减少系统中的拥堵。 “我们可以向你远离繁忙的车站，进入更积极的旅行，或者我们可以轻推你来调整你在管上旅行的时间，以便我们可以散发出服务的高峰，”客户负责人Julie Dixon说TFL的信息，设计和伙伴关系。
  L.A. Metro表示正在努力改善捕获公共汽车的经验。去年夏天推出了一个功能，它显示过境应用程序用户的公共汽车可能是多么拥挤。接下来，它计划试用“救援骑行”系统，由此如果在等待的总线被取消，则会自动建议将超级传输方式 - 例如在超级轿厢中乘坐。
  有些人说抛光数字经验可能不是公共交通的正确优先权，这是具有其他问题的公共交通。纽约的挑战包括老龄化和不可靠的火车，收入损失和潜在的削减服务。
    “地图很好，但现在是，地铁经常且可靠地运行基本工人并欢迎更多骑士，”过境倡导集团骑手联盟的政策和通信总监称，这是最重要的。
  MTA表示，随着数字设计和产品开发机构工作＆amp，Live Map表示没有介绍的东西有同意自由建设和维护产品。 
同样，L.A. Metro选择与私人建立的Transit应用程序合作。 在Transit和L.A. Metro之间没有兑换任何资金，但原理代理商现在鼓励车手下载应用程序并与过境团队一起乘客计数等信息。 Transit维护了应用程序的数字架构，并与原子能机构合作构建新功能。  替代方案是为了挽救APP L.A. Metro的声誉在2012年首次推出。  “App Store的其中一项评论是”五岁可能已经做到了这一点“，”L.A. Metro的Gordon先生说。 “我们所做的最好的事情是让向真正擅长的人开发Wayfinding应用程序的责任。”
note_en:
New York City’s Metropolitan Transportation Authority last fall released a digital version of its subway map designed to let riders see how the network is working at a glance: How close a train is to their station in real time, which tracks may be closed, and whether any planned work might disrupt a coming trip.
  The MTA said improving the clarity and ease of the digital navigation experience could encourage more people to ride the subway, including riders who have reverted to cars out of frustration over confusing track closures and delays. “This is a way, hopefully, of bringing those folks back into the system,” said Sarah Meyer, the agency’s chief customer officer.
  The MTA is one of several transit authorities that have introduced or revamped their passenger navigation tools over the past year.
  Transport for London, or TfL, last August introduced a new navigation and live service app called TfL Go. The Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, commonly known as L.A. Metro, last May announced a partnership with the Transit platform, which replaced the agency’s own as the city’s official trip-planning app.
    The new platforms are designed with more focus on the user experience than some predecessors, which first appeared on app stores around 2010 and were often neglected by transit agencies afterward, said Juan Matute, deputy director of the Institute of Transportation Studies at the University of California, Los Angeles. “These apps just fell out of favor and ended up being removed from the marketplace,” he said.
    The growth of travel alternatives like ride-sharing services, enabled in large part by their slick digital apps, has pushed agencies to look at the relative clunkiness of their own platforms, said John Gordon, L.A. Metro’s senior director of marketing strategy, digital and advertising.
  “The cold water that’s been splashed on the face of a lot of transit agencies over the past decade is the realization that we need to pay attention to our riders’ needs, and we need to build an experience that is competitive,” he said.
  The new platforms also must compete with popular apps such as Google Maps and Apple Maps, which for years have combined live transit data supplied by agencies with state-of-the-art user-experience design. But transit agencies can’t control how those apps work, or which modes of transportation they give priority to, their representatives said.
    Developing proprietary mapping platforms means agencies can improve the navigation experience on their own timeline, add unique new features to the platforms relatively quickly and gain direct access to user data, transit bosses said. The MTA in March, for example, added a Covid-19 vaccination site locator to its interactive map, which runs on a webpage rather than app in part so people without smart devices can access it. And the team behind TfL Go said it is considering introducing new features to help passengers better navigate the Tube’s often labyrinthine stations.
  Another benefit: Agencies can design their own apps to push users toward more sustainable modes of transportation, and reduce congestion in the system. “We could nudge you away from a busy station and into more active travel, or we could nudge you to adjust the time you’re traveling on the Tube so we can spread out the peaks in service,” said Julie Dixon, head of customer information, design and partnerships at TfL.
  The L.A. Metro said it is working to improve the experience of catching a bus. Last summer it introduced a feature that shows Transit app users how crowded their bus is likely to be. Next, it plans to pilot a “rescue ride” system, whereby alternative modes of transport—such as a ride in an Uber—are automatically suggested to a customer if the bus they are waiting for is canceled.
  Some said polishing the digital experience might not be the right priority for public transit, which has other problems. The challenges in New York include aging and unreliable trains, revenue losses, and  potential cuts to service.
    “The map is nice, but right now, it’s most important that the subway run frequently and reliably to serve essential workers and welcome more riders back on board,” said Danny Pearlstein, policy and communications director at the transit advocacy group Riders Alliance.
  The MTA said the live map cost nothing to introduce as it enlisted the digital design and product development agency Work & Co to build and maintain the product free.
  Similarly, L.A. Metro chose to partner with the privately built Transit app. No money is exchanged between Transit and L.A. Metro, but the agency now encourages riders to download the app and shares information such as passenger counts with the Transit team. Transit maintains the app’s digital architecture and builds new features in collaboration with the agency.
  The alternative was to salvage the reputation of the app L.A. Metro first launched in 2012.
  “One of the reviews on the App Store was ‘A five-year-old could have made this,” L.A. Metro’s Mr. Gordon said. “The best thing we did was hand the responsibility of developing a wayfinding app over to people who are really good at it.”

note_en:
At some point in your life, you've probably used a combination of sight and touch to find something hidden beneath your couch cushions. And for a while now, robotics researchers have tried to give their creations that same capability.  Back in 2019, a team of scientists from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) used a combination of tactile sensors and AI to allow a robot to identify objects by touch.
 A separate group of scientists from MIT has now built a machine that can find things it can't see initially. The aptly named RF Grasp depends on a wrist-mounted camera and an RF reader to hone in and pick up an object. As long an item has an RF tag on it, the robot can find it, even if it's hidden behind things like wrapping paper.
  This content is not available due to your privacy preferences. Update your settings  here to see it.
 Unlike your  typical university robotics project, there's a clear use case for this invention too. The team sees RF Grasp helping companies like Amazon further  automate and streamline their warehouses. "Perception and picking are two roadblocks in the industry today," said Associate Professor Alberto Rodriguez, one of the researchers who worked on the project.
 The team told   MIT News  the most challenging aspect of developing RF Grasp was integrating both sight and RF vision into its decision-making process. They compare the current system to how you might react to a sound in the distance by turning your head to pinpoint its source. RF Grasp will initially use its RF reader to find tagged objects, but the closer it gets to something, the more it depends on the information it collects through its camera to make a decision. Compared to a robot with only a visual system, RF Grasp can locate and pick up an object in about half as many total movements. It also has the unique ability to clean up and declutter its working space as it goes about its tasks.
 Of course, RF Grasp is only better than traditional picking robots at finding hidden objects when they're RF tagged. But that's not as much of a barrier as you might think. As  MIT News points out, there's already a trend in countries like  Japan to deploy RF tracking tags in the retail sector. In other words, the technology is already in place for RF Grasp to do its thing.
